Friends ➡️ Best Friends⁣

"We launched as friends, and we came back as best friends,” said @astro_reid when reflecting on the Artemis II crew’s historic test flight around the Moon.⁣

The Artemis II astronauts trained together for several years to make this mission a success, practicing everything from suiting up to getting the Orion spacecraft into different configurations. Going to space and seeing the far side of the Moon, however, was the ultimate bonding experience for them.⁣

Image descriptions:⁣
1 – From left to right, Artemis II Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen from the CSA (Canadian Space Agency), along with Pilot Victor Glover, Commander Reid Wiseman, and Mission Specialist Christina Koch, from NASA, greet family, friends, and NASA employees outside the Neil A. Armstrong Operations and Checkout Building at our Kennedy Space Center in Florida. They're all wearing orange spacesuits. Credit: NASA/Jim Ross⁣

2 – From left to right, CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen and NASA astronauts Christina Koch, Victor Glover, and Reid Wiseman smile at a crowd while standing on a stage after their return to Earth. They're wearing blue jumpsuits with patches on them. Wiseman holds Rise, the plush zero gravity indicator, in his left hand. Credit: NASA/Helen Arase Vargas⁣

#NASA #Moon #Besties

The astronauts. Their ride around the Moon. 
 
The Artemis II astronauts pose for a group photo after viewing their Orion spacecraft — which they named Integrity — in the well deck of USS John P. Murtha following their splashdown. 

Image description: NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman (@Astro_Reid), commander; left, Christina Koch (@Astro_Christina), mission specialist; CSA (@CanadianSpaceAgency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen (@AstroJeremy), mission specialist; and NASA astronaut Victor Glover (@AstroVicGlover), Artemis II pilot, right, pose for a group photo after viewing the Orion spacecraft in the well deck of USS John P. Murtha, Saturday, April 11, 2026, in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of California. 

Credit: NASA/Bill Ingalls

nasa
 
10 min
Earth knows its angles! 🌎

This Earth Day, we’re looking back at special views of our home captured by the Artemis II crew on their journey to the Moon. As they flew around the far side of the Moon, the crew was 252,756 miles from Earth at their farthest point — breaking the record set by the Apollo 13 mission in 1970.

No matter how far we go, we will always remember where we came from. 

Image descriptions:

1: Image of Earth bisected by the terminator line. The terminator divides Earth’s surface diagonally, shrouding the top left half in darkness. Earth’s illuminated surface is mostly blue and covered in swirling white clouds.

2: Image of crescent Earth. Against the blackness of space, the shining blue and white of Earth appears like a smile.

3: Image of Earth from the Artemis II mission. Earth fills the bottom half of the image, its surface covered in blue water and swirling white clouds. Earth’s limb is a blue glow against the curvature of Earth.

4: Image of Earth from the Artemis II mission. Earth fills the top half of the image. Through the gaps in white cloud cover, green and brown land masses are visible, as is blue water.

Credit: NASA

#NASA #Earth #Artemis #EarthDay

nasa
 
1 día
This nebula is ready for its close-up!

5,000 light-years from Earth, stars are forming in the Trifid Nebula. Their powerful forces shape the clouds of dust and gas that surround them; astronomers study these clouds, and how quickly they change, to understand how stars develop over time.

Our @NASAHubble telescope captured this view of the Trifid Nebula. From its vantage point in low Earth orbit, Hubble has been observing the universe for 36 years.

Image description: A rich, cosmic landscape is split in two. On the right side, clouds of reddish-black dust billow. On the left, stars sparkle across a dark-blue background.

Credit: NASA, ESA, STScI; Image Processing: Joseph DePasquale (STScI)

#NASA #Space #Nebula

And everything under the Sun is in tune 🎶
 
This image from the eclipse during the Artemis II lunar flyby captures an intense halo of light around the Moon. Scientists are still investigating if the glow comes from interstellar dust, the solar corona, or a combination of the two.
 
Image description: A solar eclipse, captured from a camera mounted on one of the Orion spacecraft’s solar array wings during the Artemis II crew’s flyby of the Moon’s far side. The Moon is dark, save for a bright halo of white light surrounding it. The Orion spacecraft is in the leftmost half of the image, mostly shadowed.
 
Credit: NASA
 
#NASA #Artemis #Eclipse #PinkFloyd

nasajohnson
 
1 sem
Wheels down on a mission generations waited for. 🛬

After completing their historic journey around the Moon on Friday, April 10 at 8:07pm ET, the @nasaartemis II crew returned to Ellington Field on Saturday, April 11, marking the final milestone of their mission. Teams across @NASA came together for this moment, supporting the crew as they transitioned from recovery operations to post‑flight evaluations and debriefs.

Their arrival closed out the first crewed Artemis mission and brought home the astronauts who helped pave the way for future lunar exploration. From launch to splashdown to the final touchdown in Houston, Artemis II represents a major step toward establishing a long‑term human presence on and around the Moon.

Ten days in space. Endless human moments. And we heard it all. 

From awe to humor, from grief to love, the Artemis II crew experienced the full range of emotion on their trip to the Moon and back. We captured a few of our favorite quotes from the mission. 

What was your favorite moment?
